ground_truth.title: How to Build an Exercise Plan - HelpGuide.org
ground_truth.author: Harvard Health
ground_truth.main_content: Looking to start exercising? Learn about the components of a balanced exercise program and explore suggestions to get you going. Regular exercise is one of the best things you can do for your physical and mental health. It can help you lose weight, improve your mood, boost your energy, and reduce your risk of chronic diseases. But starting an exercise program can feel overwhelming. You may wonder what types of exercises you should do, how often you should do them, and how to fit them into your busy schedule. The good news is that you don't need to spend hours in a gym or become a marathon runner to reap the benefits of exercise. Even moderate amounts of physical activity can make a big difference in your health and well-being. A well-rounded exercise program includes four key components: aerobic exercise, strength training, balance exercises, and flexibility exercises. Each type of exercise offers unique benefits for your body and mind. Aerobic exercise, also known as cardio, is any activity that raises your heart rate and gets you breathing harder. Examples include walking, jogging, cycling, swimming, dancing, and playing sports. Aerobic exercise strengthens your heart and lungs, improves circulation, boosts mood, and helps with weight management. Aim for at least 150 minutes of moderate aerobic activity or 75 minutes of vigorous activity per week. Strength training involves using resistance to build muscle strength and endurance. This can be done with free weights, weight machines, resistance bands, or your own body weight. Strength training helps increase bone density, improve metabolism, enhance functional strength, and prevent age-related muscle loss. Include strength training exercises at least two days per week. Balance exercises become increasingly important as you age, but people of all ages can benefit from them. These exercises help prevent falls and improve coordination. Examples include tai chi, yoga, single-leg stands, and heel-to-toe walking. Flexibility exercises help maintain range of motion in your joints and reduce the risk of injury. Stretching, yoga, and Pilates are all great options for improving flexibility. Stretch after each workout when your muscles are warm. To build an exercise plan that works for you, start by assessing your current fitness level and setting realistic goals. Consider your schedule and find ways to incorporate physical activity into your daily routine. Choose activities you enjoy, because you're more likely to stick with them. Start slowly and gradually increase the intensity and duration of your workouts. Mix up your routine to prevent boredom and work different muscle groups. Remember that some exercise is always better than none, so don't be too hard on yourself if you miss a workout. The key is to make physical activity a regular part of your life.
